g.data-narration: Welcome to Self-Referential Structures - the foundation of dynamic data structures in C programming.
Self-referential structures contain a pointer member pointing to the same structure type, enabling recursive data organization.
g.text: struct node *next; // Points to SAME type

g.data-narration: These structures enable dynamic memory allocation, allowing data structures to grow and shrink at runtime efficiently.
A node has two components: a data field to store values, and a pointer field to link to the next node in the sequence.
Memory is allocated on the heap using malloc(). Each node gets its own memory block, connected through pointers.
g.text: ↑ Allocates memory on heap | Returns pointer | Must cast to struct type

g.data-narration: A linked list connects nodes sequentially: each node points to the next, with NULL marking the end of the list.
To create a linked list, allocate each node with malloc(), assign data, and link nodes by setting the next pointer.
Insertion at the beginning takes O(1) time: create a new node, point it to the current head, then update the head pointer.
List traversal uses a while loop: continue until NULL is reached, accessing data and moving to next node each iteration.
g.text: temp = temp->next; // Move to next node

g.data-narration: Static allocation uses fixed stack memory with direct access, while dynamic allocation uses heap memory with sequential pointer access.
g.text: Stack memory • Fixed size • Direct access via index
Heap memory • Flexible size • Sequential access

g.data-narration: Linked lists offer O(1) insertions and deletions at the head, optimal memory usage, and flexible sizing compared to arrays.
Avoid dangling pointers by initializing next to NULL, prevent memory leaks with free(), and always check NULL before accessing.
Self-referential structures are the foundation for linked lists, enabling dynamic memory allocation and efficient data organization in C.
g.text: ✓ Self-referential: struct contains pointer to same type
✓ Dynamic: Memory allocated at runtime via malloc()
✓ Linked: Nodes connected via pointer members
✓ Memory: Requires manual free() to prevent leaks
